---
title: "Programming Fundamentals and Data I/O"
subtitle: "Learning R with Dr. Hu and His Friends Workshop Series"
author: "Yue Hu"
institute: "Department of Political Science, THU"

webr:
  packages:
    - drhur
    - lubridate
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## Outline


1. Foundational concepts
2. Basic syntax
3. Data types
4. Data attributes
5. Data output

::: {.fragment}

*Research question*: How does R help researchers store, import, and export data?

:::


## Object-Oriented Programming

- Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): organizes and encapsulates data through classes and objects
- C++, Java, Python, and R all adopt the OOP architecture
- Compared with Procedure-Oriented Programming (POP), OOP is better suited to complex systems and changing needs
- Two basic concepts: class (`class`) and object (`object`)
    - Class: a general specification for a group of objects (e.g., "horse", "staple food")
    - Object: a concrete instance of a class (e.g., "white horse", "potato")
    - Property: a feature of an object
    - Method: an action that can be applied to objects of a class
- Benefit of OOP: classify first, then operate in batch; avoids repetitive coding. Example: dirty shirts and dirty pants → "dirty laundry" class → uniformly "put into the washing machine"

## Functions: Imperatives with Postposed Adverbials

- Function: an imperative sentence with the adverbial postposed
- Mathematical sense: the `f` in `y = f(x)`
- In R, a function is a mathematical mapping and a set of executable actions
- Seen already: `c()`, `matrix()`, `mean()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: function

# Prepare data: create a vector from 1 to 10
x <- 1:10

# Apply function: compute the mean of x and assign to y
y <- mean(x)

y
```

:::

---

- R function syntax:

`<result_object_name> <- <function>(<target_data>, <condition_1>, <condition_2>, ...)`

- Meaning: apply a function to target data under given conditions, and store the result under the object name
- Equivalent to "imperative with postposed adverbial" in English

---

- Custom function: `function(<inputs>){<actions>}`
- Example: define a BMI calculator (BMI = weight / height²)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: bmi

# Define a function: tell R how to compute BMI
# Inputs: weight, height
calculateBMI <- function(weight, height) {
  weight / (height^2)
}

# Call the function: compute BMI for a person weighing 80 kg, 1.69 m tall
calculateBMI(80, 1.69)
```

:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Auto-printing" .fragment}

- Typing a function name directly triggers auto-print: the result appears in the console
- Pro: immediate visual feedback
- Con: the result is not saved, so it cannot be reused later

:::

---

- Function naming rules:
    - Must start with a letter
    - Only letters, digits, `.`, and `_` are allowed
    - Case-sensitive

::: {.callout-tip title="Why these naming rules?" .fragment}

- Start with a letter: lets the computer distinguish "names" from "numbers"
- No special characters: avoids conflict with programming symbols
- Case-sensitive: precision requirement

:::

---

- Naming conventions:
    - Verbs or verb phrases: `read_rds`, `saveRDS`
    - Nouns: `lm` (linear model), `dotwhisker`
    - Common abbreviations: `sqrt` (square root), `exp` (exponential)
- Names should be meaningful: readers should be able to guess what the function does
- Avoid overwriting built-in R functions (e.g., `mean`, `list`, `sum`)

## Packages: Collections of Functions {#sec-sub_package}

- Package: a collection of functions
- Analogy: a "toolbox" holding various tools
- Sources:
    - CRAN (Comprehensive R Archive Network): peer-reviewed, with quality assurance
    - GitHub and other open platforms: unreviewed, includes development versions

---

- Load a package: `library()` or `require()`
- `require()` additionally checks whether the package is already loaded and returns a logical value
- Installation and loading example:

```r
# Install a package from CRAN (only once)
install.packages("drhur")

# Install a package from GitHub (developer version)
remotes::install_github("sammo3182/drhur")

# Load the package (do this every time R restarts)
library(drhur)

# Use a function from the package to open the lecture
drhur("play")
```

---

- Ways to access help documentation:
    - `?function_name`: e.g., `?install.packages`
    - `help(function_name)`: equivalent usage
    - `help.search("keyword")`: search by keyword
    - `help.start()`: open the master documentation (rarely used)

::: {.callout-tip title="How long does installing a package take?" .fragment}

- Typically a few to a dozen seconds
- The first install also pulls dependencies, taking longer
- Lots of scrolling text is normal; just wait for it to finish

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 1**: Install multiple R packages at once

The argument name "packages" in `install.packages` is plural, suggesting that multiple packages can be installed at once. Consult the help documentation and figure out how to install `regioncode`, `dotwhisker`, and `drhur` in a single call.

::: { .hint exercise="install_multi" }

- Read `?install.packages` and check the `pkgs` argument
- The argument accepts a character vector
- Use `c()` to bundle multiple package names into a vector

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: install_multi
#| exercise: install_multi

# Try installing three packages at once
?install.packages

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="install_multi" }

```r
?install.packages

# Use c() to bundle the three package names into a character vector,
# then pass it to install.packages in a single call.
# This batch style is far more efficient than three separate install lines.
install.packages(c("regioncode", "dotwhisker", "drhur"))
```

:::

## Basic Properties of OOP

- Built on OOP, R has four key properties:
    - Inheritance
    - Encapsulation
    - Polymorphism
    - Safety

---

- Inheritance: a subclass inherits all features of its parent class
- Test with `inherits(object, "class_name")`
- Example: an ordered variable created by `ordered()` inherits from `factor`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: inherit

# Create a categorical variable x
x <- factor(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))
# Create an ordered variable y
y <- ordered(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))

# Check inheritance: is x a factor?
inherits(x, "factor")
# Check inheritance: is y a factor?
inherits(y, "factor")
```

:::

---

- Encapsulation: bundling a series of commands into a single command combo
- When called, R runs through all packaged steps in order
- `function()` is the most common encapsulation tool

::: {.callout-warning title="Encapsulation can be overwritten" .fragment}

- In R, functions are also objects: "everything can be a function"
- Existing functions can be overwritten, including built-in ones
- Dangerous example: `mean <- function(x){x^2}` turns every call to `mean()` into a squaring operation
- Be careful when overwriting, modifying, or deleting built-in commands

:::

---

- Polymorphism: the same function handles different objects differently
- Classic example: `summary()` — teaches according to the student

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: polymorphism

# Prepare data: a categorical vector x and a numeric vector y
x <- factor(c("A", "B", "C", "D"))
y <- 1:10

# summary on a factor: frequency counts per category
summary(x)

# summary on a numeric vector: mean, extremes, and other descriptives
summary(y)
```

:::

---

- Safety: a command first checks class compatibility before acting
- If incompatible, execution stops with an error and subsequent commands are not run

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: safety
#| error: true

# Error demo: try adding a number and a string
5 + "hello"

# This line will not be executed
1 + 1
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-important title="Command, method, function" .fragment}

- *Command*: any executable line of code (e.g., `1 + 1`, `y <- sqrt(x)`)
- *Function*: a fixed set of operations that takes input and returns output
- *Method*: a function tailored to objects of a particular class

:::

- A core OOP principle: *never touch the raw data directly*
- Process data via the "object + command" pattern
- Benefits: every step is traceable, safer, easier to collaborate on

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 2**: Interpret the polymorphism of `plot`

Run the commands below and explain, from a polymorphism perspective, why the same `plot` function produces different plots.

::: { .hint exercise="plot_polymorphism" }

- The number of input arguments determines `plot`'s behavior
- One argument → default to index on the X axis
- Two arguments → build a scatterplot from the pair

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: plot_polymorphism
#| exercise: plot_polymorphism

plot(mtcars$mpg)

plot(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$wt)

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="plot_polymorphism" }

```r
# One-argument call: index (1, 2, 3, ...) on the X axis, vector values on Y
plot(mtcars$mpg)

# Two-argument call: first vector on X, second on Y -> scatterplot
plot(mtcars$mpg, mtcars$wt)

# The plot function bundles multiple internal routines for different inputs.
# It intelligently picks the best one based on the number and types of arguments.
# This is polymorphism in action.
```

:::

## Assignment (Creation) {.smaller}

- Creating data in R = converting real-world observations into an analyzable form
- R records data by class; each class has its own properties and methods
- Common classes: vector, data frame, list
- Assign: give a value or object a name and cache it for later use
- The most common assignment operator: `<-`
- Equivalent commands: `assign()`, `->`, `<<-`, `=`
- Basic syntax: `<name> <- <object>`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: object

# Assignment: store the sequence 1-5 into the variable aValidObject
aValidObject <- 1:5

# Print: inspect the variable's contents
aValidObject
```

:::

---

- `<-` and `->` point in opposite directions; the side the arrow points to is the assigned name

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: arrow

# Conventional assignment: store 12 into a
a <- 12

# Reverse assignment: store 25 into b
25 -> b
```

:::

---

- `=` can also assign, but differs slightly from `<-`:
    - At the top level: identical scope
    - Inside function call parentheses: `=` is mostly for naming arguments; `<-` is mostly for creating/modifying objects

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: diffAssign
#| error: true

# Using <- : y is created in the global environment; printing y works
median(y <- 1:10); y

# Using = : x exists only inside median(); printing x outside throws an error
median(x = 1:10); x
```

:::

---

- `<<-` is used to reference/modify a parent (global) variable
- Useful when environments are nested and scopes need to be distinguished
- Example: campus bulletin (global) vs. personal reminder (local)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: globalAssign

# Campus bulletin (global variable)
campus_bulletin <- "Tonight's lecture is at 7 PM in the main hall."

# Personal reminder function: uses <- to create a local variable only, no global effect
write_personal_reminder <- function() {
  campus_bulletin <- "Finish homework before the lecture!"
  message("Personal reminder: ", campus_bulletin)
}

# Bulletin update function: uses <<- to modify the global variable
update_campus_bulletin <- function() {
  campus_bulletin <<- "LECTURE CANCELED: The professor is sick. Free time tonight!"
  message("Campus bulletin has been updated.")
}

# Inspect the bulletin
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)

# Write a personal reminder (no impact on global)
write_personal_reminder()
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)

# Update the global bulletin
update_campus_bulletin()
message("Bulletin: ", campus_bulletin)
```

:::

---

- Object naming rules:
    - Must start with a letter or `.` (if it starts with `.`, the next character cannot be a digit)
    - Cannot start with a digit
    - No special characters other than `.` and `_`
    - Case-sensitive: `X != x`
    - Beware of overwriting built-in commands (e.g., `list`, `mean`)

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 3**: Naming rules

The first line below throws an error and the second does not. Run them and explain why.

::: { .hint exercise="naming" }

- One R naming rule: names cannot start with a digit
- Which rule does the first name break?
- Is the second name compliant?

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: naming
#| exercise: naming

5var_name <- 1:5

var_name5 <- 1:5

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="naming" }

```r
# Line 1: 5var_name <- 1:5
# Error reason: R does not allow variable names to start with a digit.
# "5var_name" begins with the digit 5, which violates the naming rule.

# Line 2: var_name5 <- 1:5
# Valid: starts with the letter v and contains only letters, digits, and underscores.
var_name5
```

:::

## Vectors {.smaller}

- Vector: R's general term for any "string" of data
- Created with `c()` or `vector()`
- Strict homogeneity: elements within a single vector must share one type
- Subtypes: numeric, character, logical, factor, time (POSIXct/POSIXlt)

---

- Numeric vector: numeric values that support algebraic operations
- Sub-subtypes: double (with decimals), integer
- `NA` (not available): missing value, the "exception" of an otherwise homogeneous R vector

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: numeric

# Double-precision vector
vec_double <- c(1.5, -2.34, 1/3)

# Integer vector
vec_integer <- c(1, -2, 4, 6)

# Generate a sequence
vec_1_100 <- c(1:100)

# Vector containing missing values (NA)
vec_miss <- c(1, NA, 3, NA, 24)
```

:::

---

- Logical vector: binary `TRUE/FALSE` (abbreviated `T/F`)
- Extremely widely used: a lot of "programming" is essentially "deciding true or false"
- `as.logical()` coerces to logical type

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: logic

# Create a numeric vector (0 = FALSE, non-zero = TRUE)
vec_num <- c(1, 2, 0, 1, 1)

# Coerce to logical
vec_logic <- as.logical(vec_num)

# Inspect the result
vec_logic
```

:::

---

- Factor vector: records categorical information
- Created with `factor()`; levels record the categories, labels name them
- Subtype: ordered (ordinal) vector created by `ordered()`, with order among levels
- Advanced tool: the `forcats` package specializes in categorical/ordinal variables (reorder levels, add categories, set reference group)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: factor

# Basic factor: categories auto-detected (1, 2, 3)
vec_fac <- factor(c(1, 2, 2, 3))

# Advanced factor: custom levels (order) and labels
vec_fac2 <- factor(
  c(1, 2, 2, 3),
  levels = c(3, 2, 1),
  labels = c("Apple", "Pear", "Orange")
)

# Ordered factor: implicit ordering
vec_ord <- ordered(c(1, 2, 2, 3))

vec_fac
vec_fac2
vec_ord
```

:::

---

- Character vector: records values in character form
- R does not "understand" character meanings; it treats Chinese, English, Russian, etc. alike
- Each character value must be wrapped in quotes when created
- Built-in character constants: `letters`, `LETTERS`, `month.abb`, `month.name`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: character

# Create a character vector (note the quotation marks)
vec_chr <- c("ox", "♥️", "R is hard, but I can nail it.")

vec_chr
```

:::

---

- Time vector: R provides two flavors, `POSIXct` and `POSIXlt`
- `POSIXct`: stored as seconds (since 1970-01-01)
- `POSIXlt`: stored as a list of time components
- Current time: `Sys.time()`, `Sys.Date()`, `Sys.timezone()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: time

# Create a POSIXct time object (stored as seconds)
vector_ct <- as.POSIXct("2023-03-20 10:11:12")

# Create a POSIXlt time object (stored as a list)
vector_lt <- as.POSIXlt("2023-03-20 10:11:12")

# Print to compare (looks identical, but underlying storage differs greatly)
vector_ct
vector_lt

# Get current system time and date
Sys.time()
Sys.Date()
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-tip title="The `lubridate` package" .fragment}

- Specialized for time variables
- Flexibly parses year/month/day orderings, converts time zones, etc.

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: lubridate

library(lubridate)

# Parse "year-month-day"
ymd("20221016")
# Parse "month-day-year"
mdy("10-16-2022")
# Parse "day-month-year"
dmy("16/10/2022")
# Parse "year-month-day hour:minute:second" and set time zone
ymd_hms("2022-10-16 09:00:00", tz = "Etc/GMT+8")
```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 4**: Two ways to generate a letter sequence

Compare the two vectors produced below and verify whether they are exactly equal.

::: { .hint exercise="letters_compare" }

- `c()` enters characters one by one, manually
- `letters` is R's built-in lowercase-letter constant
- Use `identical()` to verify whether two vectors are exactly equal

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: letters_compare
#| exercise: letters_compare

vec_letters_raw <- c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e")

vec_letters_quick <- letters[1:5]

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="letters_compare" }

```r
vec_letters_raw <- c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e")
vec_letters_quick <- letters[1:5]

# Verify that the two are identical
identical(vec_letters_raw, vec_letters_quick)

# The results are exactly the same: both are five-element character vectors "a"-"e".
# vec_letters_raw: typed by hand, suitable for irregular characters.
# vec_letters_quick: leverages a built-in constant, more efficient and ideal for alphabet cases.
```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 5**: Parse messy date strings

`lubridate::parse_date_time()` can recognize and convert messy time strings according to a list of formats. Use this function to convert the following character vector into a standard time format:

`c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")`

::: { .hint exercise="date_parse" }

- Load the `lubridate` package
- The `orders` argument of `parse_date_time()` accepts a list of guesses
- Provide three format guesses with `c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy")`

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: date_parse
#| exercise: date_parse

library(lubridate)
raw_dates <- c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="date_parse" }

```r
library(lubridate)
raw_dates <- c("20190101", "01012019", "021901")

# The orders argument supplies a list of format guesses.
# The function tries each in turn and converts messy strings to standard POSIXct.
parsed_dates <- parse_date_time(raw_dates, orders = c("ymd", "dmy", "mdy"))
parsed_dates
```

:::

## Data Frames and Lists

- Data frame: a two-dimensional structure with equal-length columns; columns may be heterogeneous
- In new-liberal-arts practice, "data" usually refers to a data frame
- Extraction methods:
    - Coordinate style: `df[row, column]`
    - Name style: `df$column_name` (most common)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: dataframe

# Create a data frame with logical, numeric, and character columns
df_toy <- data.frame(
  female = c(FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, FALSE),
  age = c(29, 39, 38, 12),
  name = c("Iron Man", "Black Widow", "Captain Marvel", "Captain America")
)

# Print to inspect
df_toy

# Coordinate-style extraction: row 3, column 3
df_toy[3, 3]

# Name-style extraction: the 'name' column
df_toy$name
```

:::

---

- List: a heterogeneous container that holds a sequence of vectors
- Each vector's size, length, and order are unrestricted
- A data frame = a special list whose columns have equal length and align one-to-one

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: list

# Create a list containing vectors of different lengths
ls_monks <- list(
  name = c("Wukong Sun", "Sanzang Tang", "Wuneng Zhu", "Wujing Sha"),
  power = c(100, 20, 90, 40, 26, 78, 10000),
  buddha = c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, FALSE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE, TRUE)
)

# Print to inspect
ls_monks

# Extract the 'name' component
ls_monks$name
```

:::

---

::: {.callout-tip title="Multidimensional data: array" .fragment}

- Created with `array()`; each dimension holds a vector
- Rarely used in social sciences and humanities

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: array

# Create a 3x3x2 three-dimensional array
an_array <- array(c(5, 9, 3, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15), dim = c(3, 3, 2))

an_array
```

:::

## Data Attributes

- Probe-style tools: query object attributes
    - `class()` / `typeof()`: object class / storage mode in memory
    - `nchar()`: character-string length
    - `levels()`: factor levels
    - `length()`: length (elements for a vector, components for a list, columns for a data frame)
    - `nrow()` / `ncol()` / `dim()`: dimensions
    - `summary()`: summary statistics

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: probe

# Query attributes and types
class(vec_double)
typeof(vec_integer)

# Query character length and factor levels
nchar(vec_chr)
levels(vec_fac2)

# Query object length (note the three different meanings)
length(vec_double)
length(ls_monks)
length(df_toy)

# Query data frame dimensions
nrow(df_toy)
ncol(df_toy)
dim(df_toy)
```

:::

---

- `class()`: what R "sees" the object as → determines which functions apply
- `typeof()`: the object's underlying storage mode in memory
- `length()` on a data frame returns the number of columns (because it is essentially a list)
- Use `nrow()` for rows, `ncol()` for columns, `dim()` for full dimensions
- Predicate-style tools: usually start with `is` (`is.numeric()`, `is.character()`, `is.factor()`, `is.data.frame()`, `is.list()`); return a logical value indicating whether the object belongs to the type

## Exercise {.unnumbered .smaller background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 6**: Explore the wvs7 dataset

- World Value Survey (WVS): an international survey launched in 1981
- WVS7 is the seventh wave of this survey
- The `drhur` package ships a 2% sample of WVS7 (1264 observations, 24 variables), named `wvs7`

Use what you have learned to inspect its basic structure:

- **(a)** What class is the `wvs7` object itself?
- **(b)** How many rows and columns does `wvs7` have?
- **(c)** Extract the `marital` column and inspect which categorical levels it contains.
- **(d)** Use the `is.*` family of functions to verify: is `wvs7` a data frame? a list? a matrix?

::: { .hint exercise="wvs7_explore" }

- Use `class()` to query the type
- Use `dim()`, `nrow()`, `ncol()` to check dimensions
- Use `$` to extract a column
- Use `levels()` to view categorical levels
- Use `is.data.frame()`, `is.list()`, `is.matrix()` to verify

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: wvs7_explore
#| exercise: wvs7_explore

# Load the built-in dataset wvs7
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="wvs7_explore" }

```r
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# a. Class
class(wvs7)
# Result contains "tbl_df" "tbl" "data.frame"

# b. Dimensions
dim(wvs7)        # 1264   24
nrow(wvs7)
ncol(wvs7)

# c. The marital column
head(wvs7$marital)
levels(wvs7$marital)

# d. Structural verification
is.data.frame(wvs7)  # TRUE
is.list(wvs7)        # TRUE (a data frame is essentially a list)
is.matrix(wvs7)      # FALSE (unless coerced)
```

:::

## Built-in Data {#sec-sub_input .smaller}

- In practice, manually creating data is rare; usually data is imported externally
- Three ways to bring data in: built-in datasets, direct reading, reading via a package
- Built-in data: shipped with R itself or with packages
- List available datasets: `data()`
- Load one dataset: `data(<dataset_name>)`
- Load a dataset from a package: `data(<dataset_name>, package = "<package_name>")`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: loadData_builtin

# Load R's built-in mtcars dataset
data(mtcars)

# Inspect the summary
summary(mtcars)
```

:::

---

- Loading a dataset from another package requires the `package` argument

```r
# Try fetching corruption directly (fails if regioncode is not loaded)
data(corruption)

# Tell R explicitly to look inside the regioncode package
data(corruption, package = "regioncode")

summary(corruption)
```

---

- R's built-in `datasets` package: no install/load required, call directly
- Examples: `iris`, `mtcars`, `uspop`, etc.

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: load_iris

# datasets is loaded by default
data(iris)

# Inspect the first 6 rows to confirm
head(iris)
```

:::

## Reading Data Directly

- Data types supported by direct reading:
    - `.RDS`: R-native, single object
    - `.RData`: R-native, can store multiple objects
    - `.txt`: delimited tabular data
    - `.csv`: comma-separated values
- Input syntax: `<name> <- <reader_function>(<data_path>)`
- Reader functions usually start with `read`

---

```r
# Read an RDS file
df_rds <- readRDS("aDataset.rds")

# Read a TXT file (generic tabular reader)
df_txt <- read.table("D:/aDataset.txt")

# Read a CSV file
df_csv <- read.csv("./aDataset.csv")

# Load an RData workspace (no object name needed)
load("aDataset.RData")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="File name case sensitivity" .fragment}

- Windows / Mac: `.RData` and `.rdata` are case-insensitive
- Linux: strictly case-sensitive; the two are different files
- Recommendation: stick to lowercase `.rds`, `.rdata`

:::

## Reading Data via Packages

- Use dedicated packages to read other formats
- Common packages:
    - `haven`: SPSS / Stata / SAS
    - `readr`: CSV / TXT (faster)
    - `readxl`: Excel
    - `rjson`: JSON
    - `XML`: XML / HTML

---

```r
# --- Statistical software ---
library(haven)
df_spss <- read_spss("<FileName>.sav")
df_stata <- read_dta("<FileName>.dta")
df_sas <- read_sas("<FileName>.sas7bdat")

# --- Plain-text tables ---
library(readr)
df_csv <- read_csv("<FileName>.csv")
df_table <- read_table("<FileName>.txt")

# --- Excel ---
library(readxl)
df_excel <- read_excel("<FileName>.xlsx")

# --- JSON ---
library(rjson)
df_json <- fromJSON(file = "<FileName>.json")

# --- XML / HTML ---
library(XML)
df_xml <- xmlTreeParse("<url>")
df_html <- readHTMLTable("<url>", which = 3)
```

---

- The "Swiss Army knife": `import()` from the `rio` package
- Automatically detects the format; one function reads all
- Drawback: somewhat slower (it is a middleman)

```r
library(rio)
df_anything <- import("<AnyTypeOfData>")
```

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 7**: Load the built-in dataset uspop

Load the built-in dataset `uspop` and display its summary statistics.

::: { .hint exercise="uspop" }

- Use `data()` to load a built-in dataset
- Use `summary()` to display summary statistics

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: uspop
#| exercise: uspop

```

:::


::: { .solution exercise="uspop" }

```r
data(uspop)
summary(uspop)

# uspop is the U.S. population time series from 1790 to 1970.
# summary() automatically recognizes numeric data and prints:
# - Extremes: from 3.93 (1790) to 203.20 (1970)
# - Central tendency: mean 69.77, median 50.16
# - Mean > median, right-skewed (consistent with exponential population growth)
# Another demonstration of OOP polymorphism: summary() switches to descriptive-stats mode for numerics.
```

:::

## Saving as R Data

- Output syntax: `<function_name>(<object>, <file_path>, <other_options>)`
- Function names usually start with `save` or `write`
- R-native formats:
    - `.rds`: a single object, written by `saveRDS()`
    - `.RData`: multiple objects, written by `save()`
- Recommend `.rds`: easier to retrieve and manage
- Contents of `.RData` are visible only after `load()`

---

```r
# Save as a single-object RDS file
saveRDS(df_toy, file = "df_toy.rds")

# Save as an RData workspace (can hold multiple objects)
save(df_toy, ls_monks, file = "./data/test.rdata")
```

## Saving as a CSV File

- CSV: a cross-platform universal format
- Compression efficiency is low, but virtually every platform and program can read it
- Use `write.csv()` to save

```r
# Save df_toy as a CSV file
write.csv(df_toy, file = "toy.csv")
```

::: {.callout-warning title="CSV and Chinese data" .fragment}

- Save Chinese data using UTF-8 encoding
- Otherwise garbled characters may appear

:::

## Saving in Other Formats

- Use `export()` from the `rio` package
- Auto-detects the suffix and saves an R object to almost any format
- Supports STATA, SPSS, SAS, Excel, JSON, Matlab, HTML, and more

::: {.callout-tip title="Data storage efficiency" .fragment}

- Small-to-medium data: `.RData` is usually enough
- Large data: consider specialized formats like `qs` or `fst`
- Specialized formats have weaker compatibility and storage-stability risks (a different machine may fail to open them)

:::

## Recap {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="33%"}

- Foundational concepts
    - OOP (class and object)
    - Functions and assignment
    - Packages
- Data types
    - Vectors (numeric, logical, factor, character, time)
    - Matrix
    - Data frames and lists (key topic)

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}

- Data attributes: inspect data type, dimensions, and overview
- Data input
    - Loading built-in data
    - Reading external data
    - Using dedicated packages (`haven`, `rio`, etc.)
- Data output
    - Saving as R data
    - Saving in other data formats

:::

::: {.column width="33%"}

- Key functions of this chapter:
    - Basic operations and package management: `install.packages()`, `library()`, `help()` / `?`
    - Data creation and types: `c()`, `data.frame()`, `list()`, `as.numeric()`, `as.character()`, `as.logical()`
    - Data attributes: `class()`, `summary()`, `dim()`, `nrow()`, `ncol()`, `length()`, `head()`
    - Data input and export: `data()`, `read.csv()`, `readRDS()`, `saveRDS()`, `write.csv()`

:::

::::
